perc_hh_with_broadband_geo20.long_description: Broadband adoption measures the share of households with an internet connection faster than dial-up, including cable, fiber, DSL, satellite, and cellular data. Communities with low broadband adoption face barriers to remote work, online education, government services, and digital commerce — a gap often called the digital divide. Broadband access is increasingly treated as essential infrastructure, comparable to electricity or water service.
perc_hh_with_broadband_geo20.short_description: Percentage of households with any broadband internet connection (excludes dial-up).

sources.location: 5-Year estimates, Table B28002, via the API
sources.name: American Community Survey

perc_hh_with_broadband_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, Table B28002 (Presence and Types of Internet Subscriptions in Household): B28002_004 (broadband of any type) / B28002_001 (total households) × 100. Broadband is defined by the Census Bureau as any internet subscription type other than dial-up.
perc_hh_with_broadband_geo20.statement: {value}% of households in {features.name} have a broadband connection.

perc_hh_with_cable_fiber_dsl_geo20.long_description: High-speed wired internet adoption measures the share of households connected via cable, fiber optic, or DSL — the connection types that deliver the most reliable speeds for streaming, video calls, and large file transfers. Unlike satellite or cellular, wired connections provide consistent low-latency service. Areas with low wired broadband adoption may rely on slower or less reliable alternatives, limiting participation in bandwidth-intensive activities.
perc_hh_with_cable_fiber_dsl_geo20.long_name: Percentage of Households with Cable, Fiber, or DSL
perc_hh_with_cable_fiber_dsl_geo20.short_description: Percentage of households with cable, fiber optic, or DSL internet service.

sources.location: 5-Year estimates, Table B28002, via the API
sources.name: American Community Survey

perc_hh_with_cable_fiber_dsl_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, Table B28002: B28002_007 (cable, fiber optic, or DSL subscription) / B28002_001 (total households) × 100.
perc_hh_with_cable_fiber_dsl_geo20.statement: {value}% of households in {features.name} have a cable, fiber, or DSL connection.

perc_hh_without_internet_geo20.long_description: The share of households with no internet access at home — neither broadband, dial-up, nor cellular data. These households are fully disconnected from online services, unable to access government portals, apply for jobs electronically, or participate in remote education. High rates of disconnection often correlate with poverty, older populations, and rural geography, making this a key indicator of digital equity.
perc_hh_without_internet_geo20.long_name: Percentage of Households without Internet Access
perc_hh_without_internet_geo20.short_description: Percentage of households with no internet access of any kind at home.

perc_hh_without_internet_geo20.statement: {value}% of households in {features.name} do not have internet access.

avg_down_speed_geo20.long_description: Device-weighted average fixed broadband download speed in megabits per second (Mb/s), derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups. Each tile's average download speed (avg_d_kbps) is weighted by the number of unique devices tested. Quarterly block group values are averaged within each year. Tract and county values are simple means of their constituent block groups.
avg_down_speed_geo20.provenance: Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles. Tiles are spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups using intersection. Per-tile average download speed (avg_d_kbps field) is aggregated to block groups using device-weighted means. Quarterly values are averaged within each year. Tract and county values are simple means of constituent block groups.
avg_down_speed_geo20.short_description: Average download speed weighted by number of devices.
avg_down_speed_geo20.statement: The average download speed is {value} Mb/s in {region_name}.

perc_above_25_3_geo20.long_description: Percentage of tested devices with download speed at or above 25 Mb/s and upload speed at or above 3 Mb/s, the FCC's former broadband threshold. Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups. At the tile level, a tile meets the threshold if its average download speed >= 25,000 kbps and average upload speed >= 3,000 kbps. The percentage is calculated as the share of devices in qualifying tiles relative to total devices in each block group.

As a component of the VDH Health Opportunity Index Built Environment profile, this measure helps characterize the stability and cohesion of a community.

Block group estimates produced via areal interpolation from tract-level data.

population_density_geo20.long_description: Population density measures the number of people living per square mile in a geographic area. Dense communities typically have greater access to services, transit, and employment, but may also face challenges such as housing pressure, environmental exposure, and infrastructure strain. As a component of the VDH Health Opportunity Index Built Environment profile, density helps characterize whether a community is urban, suburban, or rural.

Land area from Census TIGER/Line shapefiles (ALAND field, converted from square meters to square miles). Density calculated as total population divided by land area in square miles at tract, county, and block group levels.

segregation_indicator_geo20.citations: Theil, H. (1972). Statistical Decomposition Analysis. Amsterdam: North-Holland Publishing Company.; Reardon, S. F., & Firebaugh, G. (2002). Measures of Multigroup Segregation. Sociological Methodology, 32(1), 33–67.
segregation_indicator_geo20.long_description: The Segregation Index measures how much a community's racial and ethnic composition differs from the state as a whole, using the Entropy Index (Theil's H) across eight racial/ethnic categories. Communities with high segregation values tend to have more concentrated poverty, unequal access to services, and wider disparities in educational and economic outcomes. As a component of the VDH Health Opportunity Index Social Impact profile, this measure helps identify areas where residential sorting by race may reinforce structural inequities.
segregation_indicator_geo20.short_description: Entropy Index (Theil's H) measuring how sub-area racial/ethnic composition differs from the overall state composition.

sources.name: U.S. Census Bureau
sources.location: ACS 5-Year Estimates, Table B03002 (Hispanic or Latino Origin by Race)

segregation_indicator_geo20.provenance: The segregation indicator uses the Entropy Index (Theil's H), a method for measuring multigroup residential segregation that quantifies how sub-area racial/ethnic composition differs from the overall composition (Theil, 1972; Reardon & Firebaugh, 2002). This pipeline builds on methodology developed by the Virginia Department of Health, Office of Minority Health and Health Equity (VDH-OMHHE) for the Health Opportunity Index (HOI), which includes a segregation indicator as one of its 13 community health profiles.

veteran_count_geo20.long_description: The veteran population count captures the number of civilians aged 18 and older who have served in the U.S. armed forces. Communities with large veteran populations have greater demand for VA health services, disability support, mental health care, and employment transition programs. Tracking veteran counts over time reveals migration patterns as veterans relocate for benefits access, cost of living, or proximity to military installations. This measure complements the veteran percentage by providing the absolute scale needed to plan service capacity and allocate resources.
veteran_count_geo20.provenance: Directly tabulated from ACS 5-year estimates, table B21001 (Sex by Age by Veteran Status for the Civilian Population 18 Years and Over). Veteran count is B21001_002; percentage calculated relative to civilian population 18 and over (B21001_001).

daycare_capacity.long_description: Total licensed day care capacity measures the number of available childcare seats in a region. Insufficient childcare capacity constrains parents' ability to work, disproportionately affecting women's labor force participation and low-income families who cannot afford informal care alternatives. This raw capacity count complements per-capita ratios by showing the absolute supply of childcare available.
daycare_capacity.short_description: Total number of licensed day care seats in the region, from VDSS facility records.
daycare_capacity.provenance: Total licensed day care capacity reported by the Virginia Department of Social Services, summed within each geographic region.
daycare_capacity.statement: There are {value} day care seats in {region_name}.

daycare_min_drivetime.long_description: Time (minutes) to drive to the nearest day care provider of any type. Times are calculated using the Open Source Routing Machine, from block group centroids to each provider. Times for higher geographic levels are averaged across block groups.
daycare_min_drivetime.short_description: Drive time in minutes to the nearest day care provider, based on OSRM routing from block group centroids.
daycare_min_drivetime.provenance: Minimum drive time from block group centroids to day care providers, calculated using the Open Source Routing Machine with pre-computed BG-to-BG travel times.
daycare_min_drivetime.statement: It takes {value} to drive to the nearest day care provider in {region_name}.

daycare_ratio.long_description: Number of day care seats per 1,000 children under 15 years of age, which accept at least ages 4 to 10, as calculated within floating catchment areas. Catchment area ratios are based on the population of children under 15 within block groups (as estimated in the American Community Survey), capacity of eligible day care providers (as reported by the Virginia Department of Social Services), and travel times between block group centroids and day care locations (as calculated with the Open Source Routing Machine). Catchment areas are weighted by travel time with a Gaussian function with scale of 18, with quadratic selection-weight normalization (Wan et al., 2012) for the 3-step floating catchment area.
daycare_ratio.long_name: Day Care Seats Per 1k Children Under 15, Accepting at Least Ages 4-10
daycare_ratio.short_description: Day care seats per 1,000 children under 15, using a 3-step floating catchment area method with Gaussian distance-decay weighting.
daycare_ratio.provenance: Day care availability ratios are calculated using the 3-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with quadratic selection-weight normalization (Wan et al., 2012) and Gaussian distance-decay (scale parameter 18/sqrt(2) approx 12.73).
daycare_ratio.statement: There are {value} day care seats per 1,000 children under 15 in {region_name}.

daycare_ratio_over_4.long_description: Number of day care seats per 1,000 children between 5 and 14 years of age, which accept at least some ages over 4, as calculated within floating catchment areas. Catchment area ratios are based on the population of children over 4 within block groups (as estimated in the American Community Survey), capacity of eligible day care providers (as reported by the Virginia Department of Social Services), and travel times between block group centroids and day care locations (as calculated with the Open Source Routing Machine). Catchment areas are weighted by travel time with a Gaussian function with scale of 18, with quadratic selection-weight normalization (Wan et al., 2012) for the 3-step floating catchment area.
daycare_ratio_over_4.long_name: Day Care Seats Per 1k Children Between 5 and 14, With Minimal Accepted Age Over 4
daycare_ratio_over_4.short_description: Day care seats per 1,000 children ages 5-14, using 3SFCA method. Only providers accepting ages over 4.
daycare_ratio_over_4.provenance: Day care availability ratios are calculated using the 3-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with quadratic selection-weight normalization (Wan et al., 2012) and Gaussian distance-decay (scale parameter 18/sqrt(2) approx 12.73).
daycare_ratio_over_4.statement: There are {value} day care seats per 1,000 children over 4 in {region_name}.

daycare_ratio_under_10.long_description: Number of day care seats per 1,000 children under 10 years of age, which accept at least some ages under 10, as calculated within floating catchment areas. Catchment area ratios are based on the population of children under 10 within block groups (as estimated in the American Community Survey), capacity of eligible day care providers (as reported by the Virginia Department of Social Services), and travel times between block group centroids and day care locations (as calculated with the Open Source Routing Machine). Catchment areas are weighted by travel time with a Gaussian function with scale of 18, with quadratic selection-weight normalization (Wan et al., 2012) for the 3-step floating catchment area.
daycare_ratio_under_10.long_name: Day Care Seats Per 1k Children Under 10, With Maximal Accepted Age Under 10
daycare_ratio_under_10.short_description: Day care seats per 1,000 children under 10, using 3SFCA method. Only providers accepting ages under 10.

daycare_ratio_under_10.statement: There are {value} day care seats per 1,000 children under 10 in {region_name}.

schoolFundAdequacy.long_description: School funding adequacy measures the gap between what districts actually spend per pupil and what they would need to spend to achieve national average test scores, after accounting for student demographics and regional cost differences. Persistent funding gaps concentrate in districts serving higher shares of low-income students and students of color, reinforcing cycles of educational inequity. Adequately funded schools can attract stronger teachers, offer smaller class sizes, and provide the wraparound services that improve student outcomes.
schoolFundAdequacy.short_description: Average gap in dollars between actual and required spending per pupil in school districts.
schoolFundAdequacy.provenance: School funding adequacy data is from the School Finance Indicators Database (SFID), developed by researchers at the Albert Shanker Institute, Rutgers University, and University of Miami. Required spending is estimated using a cost-function model that accounts for student demographics, regional cost differences, and grade-level concentrations.
schoolFundAdequacy.statement: The average gap between actual and required spending per pupil is {value} in {features.name}.

Higher attainment rates correlate with greater economic mobility, higher household incomes, and better health outcomes. This indicator helps identify communities that may benefit from expanded access to higher education and workforce development programs.

acs_postsecondary_percent_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, table B06009 (Place of Birth by Educational Attainment). Postsecondary education includes anyone reporting education at the associate degree level or higher.
acs_postsecondary_percent_geo20.short_description: Percentage of the population with any postsecondary education.
