mean_read_pass_rate.long_description: The mean reading pass rate provides an average measure of 3rd grade literacy across schools in a region. Unlike the median, the mean is influenced by exceptionally high- or low-performing schools, making it useful for understanding aggregate performance. Comparing mean and median pass rates can reveal whether a region's schools have broadly similar outcomes or a wide performance spread.
mean_read_pass_rate.long_name: Mean percentage of 3rd graders passing reading test
mean_read_pass_rate.provenance: Mean of school-level 3rd grade reading pass rates from Virginia Department of Education Standards of Learning (SOL) assessments. Pass rates are calculated by VDOE as the number of students passing divided by the number tested at each school. The mean is then computed across all schools within each geographic region.
mean_read_pass_rate.short_description: Mean percentage of 3rd graders passing the state reading assessment, averaged across schools in the region.

sources.name: Virginia Department of Education
sources.location: Data & Reports > Statistics & Reports > SOL Test Pass Rates & Other Results

mean_read_pass_rate.statement: On average, {value} of 3rd graders pass reading in {region_name}.

average_years_schooling_geo20.long_description: Average years of schooling estimates the typical education level of adults in a community by converting Census educational attainment categories into equivalent years of formal education. Areas with lower average schooling tend to have fewer economic opportunities, lower health literacy, and reduced civic engagement. This measure serves as a key input to the VDH Health Opportunity Index, where it contributes to the Consumer Opportunity profile.
average_years_schooling_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, table B15003 (Sex by Age by Educational Attainment for the Population 18 Years and Over). Each educational attainment category is mapped to a number of years using the crosswalk in Bennett, D.L. (2011), 'Educational Inequality in the United States,' Center for College Affordability and Productivity. The population-weighted average across all attainment levels produces a single years-of-schooling estimate per geography.
average_years_schooling_geo20.short_description: Average years of formal education among adults, derived from ACS educational attainment categories.

sources.location: 5-Year estimates, table B15001, via the API
sources.name: American Community Survey

average_years_schooling_geo20.statement: The average years of schooling in {features.name} is {value}.

environmental_hazard_index_geo20.long_description: The Environmental Hazard Index captures the cumulative burden of environmental hazards in a community by distilling 12 EPA pollution and proximity indicators into a single score. Communities with high values face greater combined exposure to air toxics, particulate matter, ozone, hazardous waste sites, and aging housing stock. The index is reported as a z-score, where zero represents the Virginia average and each unit represents one standard deviation of additional hazard burden.
environmental_hazard_index_geo20.short_description: A z-score summarizing cumulative environmental hazard exposure from 12 EPA pollution and proximity indicators.
environmental_hazard_index_geo20.provenance: Block-group-level data for 12 environmental indicators (CANCER, RESP, PTRAF, PWDIS, PNPL, PRMP, PTSDF, OZONE, PM25, PRE1960PCT, DSLPM, UST) extracted from EPA EJScreen annual CSVs (2016-2024, archived via Internet Archive). UST values backfilled from 2021 for years 2016-2020 when the variable was unavailable. Variables standardized per year, then reduced via Principal Component Analysis (1 component) using scikit-learn. PC1 scores converted to z-scores. Block groups aggregated to tracts via population-weighted mean (simple mean fallback). 2024 uses 10 variables (EPA dropped CANCER and RESP). EJScreen used 2010 census block groups through 2021 and switched to 2020 block groups in 2022. Pre-2022 tract values crosswalked from 2010 to 2020 boundaries via area-weighted redistribution.
environmental_hazard_index_geo20.statement: The environmental hazard index in {features.name} is {value}.

alice_pct.long_description: Percentage of households that are Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed. Households Earning more than the Federal Poverty Level, but not enough to afford the basics where they live.
alice_pct.long_name: Percentage of households that are Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed (ALICE)
alice_pct.short_description: The percent of households that are above the Federal Poverty Level but still cannot afford the basics where they live.
alice_pct.provenance: ALICE (Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed) is a framework developed by United for Alice (a United Way initiative) that defines a household survival budget based on local costs of housing, childcare, food, transportation, health care, and taxes.
alice_pct.statement: {value} of the households in {features.name} are ALICE.

poverty_pct.long_description: Percentage of households that are below the Federal Poverty Level. Not being able to afford the basics where they live.
poverty_pct.long_name: Percentage of households that are below the Federal Poverty Level.
poverty_pct.short_description: The percent of households that are below the Federal Poverty Level.
poverty_pct.provenance: ALICE (Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed) is a framework developed by United for Alice (a United Way initiative) that defines a household survival budget based on local costs of housing, childcare, food, transportation, health care, and taxes.
poverty_pct.statement: {value} of the households in {features.name} are in poverty.

employment_access_index_geo20.long_description: The Employment Access Index quantifies how easily residents of a community can reach jobs, using a gravity model that accounts for both the number of nearby jobs and the distance to them. Communities with low employment access face longer commutes, higher transportation costs, and fewer economic opportunities, all of which contribute to financial instability and reduced quality of life. This measure is a key input to the VDH Health Opportunity Index, where it contributes to the Economic Opportunity profile.
employment_access_index_geo20.provenance: Computed using a gravity model from LEHD-LODES Workplace Area Characteristics (WAC) job counts and TIGER/Line Census block centroids. Employment intensity is calculated as the sum of jobs divided by the square of the distance (in miles) from each Census block group to all nearby employment locations, using a hierarchical distance approximation: individual block-level job counts within 34 miles, tract-level aggregates from 34 to 165 miles, and county-level aggregates from 165 to 200 miles. Inspired by the Center for Neighborhood Technology (CNT) H+T Affordability Index gravity model approach.
employment_access_index_geo20.short_description: Gravity-based index of employment accessibility measuring proximity to jobs, weighted by distance decay.

sources.location: LODES WAC S000 JT00, column C000 (total jobs per Census block)
sources.name: LEHD-LODES Workplace Area Characteristics

sources.location: TABBLOCK20, BG, TRACT, COUNTY shapefiles (INTPTLAT, INTPTLON fields)
sources.name: TIGER/Line Shapefiles (2020 Census Geography)

employment_access_index_geo20.statement: The employment access index for {features.name} is {value}.

labor_participate_rate_geo20.long_description: Labor force participation measures the share of working-age adults (16-64) who are either employed or actively seeking work. Low participation rates may indicate discouraged workers who have stopped looking for employment, high rates of disability, or populations engaged in unpaid caregiving. Economists consider participation rates a more complete picture of economic engagement than employment rates alone.
labor_participate_rate_geo20.provenance: From ACS 5-year estimates, table S2301 (Employment Status), variable S2301_C02_001 (civilian labor force participation rate for the population 16-64 years).
labor_participate_rate_geo20.short_description: Percentage of individuals aged 16-64 participating in the civilian labor force.

sources.name: American Community Survey
sources.location: 5-Year estimates, table S2301_C02_001, via the API

labor_participate_rate_geo20.statement: {value} of the population in {region_name} is participating in the civilian job force.

emp_rate_geo20.long_description: The employment rate measures the share of the civilian labor force that is currently working. High employment rates indicate a healthy local labor market with sufficient job opportunities. Low rates may reflect structural economic challenges, skills mismatches, or barriers to employment such as transportation or childcare access.
emp_rate_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, table B23025 (Employment Status for the Population 16 Years and Over). Employment rate calculated as B23025_004 (employed) / B23025_003 (civilian labor force) × 100.
emp_rate_geo20.short_description: Percentage of the civilian workforce population that is employed.

sources.name: American Community Survey
sources.location: 5-Year estimates, table B23025, via the API

emp_rate_geo20.statement: {value} of the population in {region_name} is employed.

median_household_income_geo20.long_description: Median household income represents the midpoint of all household incomes in a community — half earn more, half earn less. It is one of the most widely used indicators of economic well-being, reflecting local employment opportunities, wage levels, and cost-of-living dynamics. Communities with low median incomes often face compounding challenges in housing affordability, food security, and access to services.
median_household_income_geo20.provenance: From ACS 5-year estimates, table B19013 (Median Household Income in the Past 12 Months, in Inflation-Adjusted Dollars). Values are adjusted to the survey year's constant dollars by the Census Bureau.
median_household_income_geo20.short_description: Median household income in inflation-adjusted dollars.
median_household_income_geo20.statement: The median household income in {features.name} is {value} per year.

gini_index_geo20.long_description: The Gini Index is a summary measure of income inequality ranging from 0 (perfect equality, where everyone receives an equal share) to 1 (perfect inequality, where one household receives all income). Communities with high Gini values have large gaps between the highest and lowest earners, which is associated with reduced social mobility, poorer population health outcomes, and weaker community cohesion. This measure is a key input to the VDH Health Opportunity Index, where it contributes to the Economic Opportunity profile.
gini_index_geo20.provenance: Published directly by the U.S. Census Bureau in American Community Survey 5-year estimates, Table B19083 (Gini Index of Income Inequality). The Gini coefficient is computed by the Census Bureau from the observed cumulative household income distribution (Lorenz curve) relative to a perfectly equal distribution. Originally developed by Corrado Gini (1912).
gini_index_geo20.short_description: Gini Index of income inequality (0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality), from ACS Table B19083.

sources.location: 5-Year estimates, Table B19083, via the API
sources.name: American Community Survey

gini_index_geo20.statement: The income inequality (Gini Index) in {features.name} is {value}.

material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.citations: Townsend, P., Phillimore, P., & Beattie, A. (1988). Health and Deprivation: Inequality and the North. London: Croom Helm.
material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.long_description: The Material Deprivation Index captures the extent to which a community lacks basic material resources — stable employment, adequate housing space, vehicle access, and home ownership. Communities with high deprivation scores face compounding disadvantages: unemployment limits income, overcrowding increases disease transmission and stress, lack of vehicle access restricts mobility, and renting reduces wealth accumulation. The index is scaled from 0 (least deprived) to 1 (most deprived) and serves as a key input to the VDH Health Opportunity Index, where it contributes to the Consumer Opportunity profile.
material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.short_description: Townsend Material Deprivation Index (0-1) combining unemployment, overcrowding, non-car ownership, and non-home ownership.

sources.name: U.S. Census Bureau
sources.location: ACS 5-Year Estimates, Tables B23025 (Employment Status), B25014 (Tenure by Occupants per Room), B25044 (Tenure by Vehicles Available), and S2502 (Demographic Characteristics for Occupied Housing Units)

material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.provenance: Based on the Townsend Material Deprivation Index (Townsend, Phillimore & Beattie, 1988), originally developed for the UK Census to measure area-level material deprivation. This pipeline adapts the methodology for U.S. Census ACS variables. The four component indicators (unemployment, overcrowding, non-car ownership, non-home ownership) are computed from ACS 5-year estimates, z-scored within year and geography level, summed, z-scored again, and min-max rescaled to [0, 1]. The VDH Health Opportunity Index includes a similar adaptation of the Townsend index as one of its 13 indicators.
material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.statement: The material deprivation indicator in {features.name} is {value}.

earnings_per_job.long_description: Earnings per job measures the average compensation across all jobs in a county, combining wages, salary supplements (like employer-provided health insurance and retirement contributions), and proprietor’s income. This metric reflects local economic productivity and the quality of available employment. Counties with low earnings per job may have economies dominated by lower-wage service or retail sectors.
earnings_per_job.provenance: Computed from Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) GDP and Personal Income tables. Earnings per job = total earnings / total number of jobs, where total earnings includes wages and salaries, supplements to wages and salaries, and proprietor’s income.
earnings_per_job.short_description: Average earnings per job, including wages, salaries, supplements, and proprietor’s income.

food_access_percentage_geo20.long_description: Food access measures the share of a tract's population that is both low-income and lives more than one mile from a supermarket. Tracts with high values have larger populations facing simultaneous barriers of poverty and geographic distance to healthy food retailers, which is associated with poorer diet quality and higher rates of diet-related chronic disease.
food_access_percentage_geo20.short_description: Share of low-income population beyond one mile from a supermarket.
food_access_percentage_geo20.provenance: Tract-level low-access, low-income population shares (lalowi1share, with lalowihalfshare as fallback) extracted from USDA Food Access Research Atlas 2015 and 2019 Excel editions. Low-income defined by Department of Treasury NMTC criteria (poverty rate >= 20%, or median family income <= 80% of state/metro median). Values for 2016-2018 linearly interpolated between 2015 and 2019 observations. Values for 2020-2023 linearly extrapolated from the 2015-2019 trend. Original FARA data uses 2010 census tracts; values crosswalked to 2020 boundaries via area-weighted redistribution using the Census Bureau tract relationship file.
food_access_percentage_geo20.statement: {value} percent of the low-income population in {features.name} lives beyond one mile from a supermarket.

Child_Food_Insecurity_Rate.long_description: The percentage of food insecure children. Food insecurity refers to the US Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) measure of lack of access, at times, to enough food for an active, healthy life for all household members and limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate foods. Food insecure children are those children living in households that are not necessarily food insecure all the time. Food insecurity may reflect a household’s need to make trade-offs between important basic needs, such as housing or medical bills, and purchasing nutritionally adequate foods. Geographies: County (5)
Child_Food_Insecurity_Rate.provenance: Child food insecurity estimates are produced by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, which uses a regression model applied to Current Population Survey and American Community Survey data to generate county-level food insecurity rates.
Child_Food_Insecurity_Rate.statement: {value} of the population of children in {features.name} are food insecure.

Num_Child_Food_Insecure.long_description: The number of food insecure children. Food insecurity refers to the US Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) measure of lack of access, at times, to enough food for an active, healthy life for all household members and limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate foods. Food insecure children are those children living in households that are not necessarily food insecure all the time. Food insecurity may reflect a household’s need to make trade-offs between important basic needs, such as housing or medical bills, and purchasing nutritionally adequate foods. Geographies: County (5)
Num_Child_Food_Insecure.provenance: Child food insecurity estimates are produced by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, which uses a regression model applied to Current Population Survey and American Community Survey data to generate county-level food insecurity rates.
Num_Child_Food_Insecure.statement: The population of food insecure children in {features.name} is {value}.

weighted_budget_shortfall.provenance: Food budget shortfall is estimated by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, calculated as the difference between estimated food spending by food-insecure individuals and the cost of a modestly priced meal plan.

Cost_Per_Meal.long_description: The average weekly dollar amount food-secure individuals report spending on food, as estimated by the Current Population Survey, divided by 21 (assuming three meals a day for seven days a week). This amount has been adjusted to reflect local food prices and relevant taxes. Geographies: County (5)
Cost_Per_Meal.provenance: Average meal cost is estimated by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, derived from Current Population Survey self-reported food spending among food-secure individuals, adjusted for local food prices.
Cost_Per_Meal.statement: The average meal cost in {features.name} is {value}.

number_food_insecure.long_description: Estimated number of total people in food insecure households according to Feeding America. Food insecurity refers to the US Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) measure of lack of access, at times, to enough food for an active, healthy life for all household members and limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate foods. The brief providing the methodology of calculating these estimates is available on Feeding America’s Map the Meal Gap website. Food insecurity may reflect a household’s need to make trade-offs between important basic needs, such as housing or medical bills, and purchasing nutritionally adequate foods. Geographies: Census tract (11), County (5), Health Districts (51_hd)
number_food_insecure.long_name: Estimated number of total people in food insecure households
number_food_insecure.provenance: Food insecurity estimates are produced by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, which uses a regression model applied to Current Population Survey and American Community Survey data to generate county-level food insecurity rates.

percent_food_insecure.long_description: Estimated percentage of total people in food insecure households according to Feeding America. Food insecurity refers to the US Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) measure of lack of access, at times, to enough food for an active, healthy life for all household members and limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate foods. The brief providing the methodology of calculating these estimates is available on Feeding America’s Map the Meal Gap website. Food insecurity may reflect a household’s need to make trade-offs between important basic needs, such as housing or medical bills, and purchasing nutritionally adequate foods. Geographies: Census tract (11), County (5), Health Districts (51_hd)
percent_food_insecure.long_name: Estimated percentage of total people in food insecure households
percent_food_insecure.provenance: Food insecurity estimates are produced by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, which uses a regression model applied to Current Population Survey and American Community Survey data to generate county-level food insecurity rates.
percent_food_insecure.statement: {value} percent of the population in {features.name} are food insecure.

hh_received_snap_cnt.long_description: The count of households receiving Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits reflects the scale of food assistance need in a community. SNAP participation correlates with poverty, food insecurity, and limited access to affordable nutrition. High counts help size the demand for complementary food assistance programs such as food banks and community meal sites.
hh_received_snap_cnt.long_name: Count of Households That Received Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
hh_received_snap_cnt.provenance: From ACS 5-year estimates, table B22010 (Receipt of Food Stamps/SNAP in the Past 12 Months by Household Type). Count of households where at least one member received SNAP benefits. Does not include households receiving only WIC, school lunch, or food bank assistance.
hh_received_snap_cnt.short_description: Number of households with at least one member receiving SNAP benefits in the past 12 months.
hh_received_snap_cnt.short_name: Count of Households That Received SNAP
hh_received_snap_cnt.statement: The count of households that received SNAP in {features.name} is {value}.

hh_received_snap_pct.long_description: The SNAP participation rate measures the share of households relying on federal food assistance. Higher rates indicate communities with greater economic hardship and food insecurity. This measure is useful for targeting nutrition assistance programs and identifying areas where food access interventions may have the greatest impact.
hh_received_snap_pct.long_name: Percentage of Households That Received Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
hh_received_snap_pct.provenance: From ACS 5-year estimates, table B22010 (Receipt of Food Stamps/SNAP in the Past 12 Months by Household Type). Percentage of total households where at least one member received SNAP benefits.
hh_received_snap_pct.short_description: Percentage of households with at least one member receiving SNAP benefits in the past 12 months.
hh_received_snap_pct.short_name: Percentage of Households That Received SNAP
hh_received_snap_pct.statement: The percentage of households that received SNAP in {features.name} is {value}.

population.long_description: The total household count provides the denominator for SNAP participation rate calculations and reflects the overall size of the residential population at the household level. This baseline count is essential for contextualizing food assistance metrics.
population.provenance: From ACS 5-year estimates, table B22010 (Receipt of Food Stamps/SNAP in the Past 12 Months by Household Type). Total household universe for the SNAP participation question.
population.short_description: Total number of households in the geographic area.
population.statement: The number of households in {features.name} is {value}.

access_care_indicator_geo20.long_description: Quintile indicator (1–5) measuring access to primary care, where 1 = worst access and 5 = best access. Derived from a composite z-score combining two components: (1) population-to-physician ratio, using CMS Medicare Physician PUF data to count primary care physicians (Internal Medicine, Family Practice, Pediatric Medicine, OB/GYN) within 30 miles of each census tract via OSRM driving distances and HUD ZIP-to-tract crosswalk allocation; and (2) percentage of uninsured residents aged 19-64 from ACS 5-year estimates (B27010). The underlying z-score is computed as -1 × z(z(pop_per_physician) + z(pct_uninsured)), then converted to quintiles per year across all Virginia census tracts. County and health district values are the mean of their constituent tract quintiles.
access_care_indicator_geo20.short_description: Quintile indicator (1-5) of physician availability within 30 miles and insurance coverage rate, where 5 = best access.

sources.name: CMS Medicare Physician & Other Practitioners PUF
sources.location: Medicare Physician & Other Practitioners - by Provider, 2017-2023

sources.name: U.S. Census Bureau
sources.location: ACS 5-Year Estimates, Tables B01001 (Total Population) and B27010 (Health Insurance Coverage)

sources.name: HUD Office of Policy Development and Research
sources.location: USPS ZIP Code-Census Tract Crosswalk, December 2021

sources.name: OSRM (Open Source Routing Machine)
sources.location: Block-group-to-block-group driving distances, 8-state region (VA, MD, DC, DE, NC, TN, KY, WV)

access_care_indicator_geo20.provenance: Based on the Access to Care methodology originally developed by the Virginia Department of Health (VDH), Office of Minority Health and Health Equity (OMHHE) as part of their Health Opportunity Index (HOI). This pipeline reproduces and extends the VDH indicator using open data sources: CMS Medicare Physician PUF (primary care providers in VA, assigned to census tracts via HUD ZIP-to-tract crosswalk with residential ratio weighting) and ACS 5-year uninsured estimates. Physician accessibility uses OSRM-based driving distances between block group centroids, aggregated to tract level, with a 30-mile threshold. The composite z-score is the negated z-score of the sum of z-scored population-per-physician ratio and z-scored uninsured percentage, then converted to quintiles (1-5) per year to match the VDH HOI format.
access_care_indicator_geo20.statement: The access to care indicator in {features.name} is {value}.

substance_near_10_median.long_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten closest substance abuse treatment facilities. Communities with longer drive times to treatment face significant barriers to accessing care, which can delay intervention during critical moments of readiness for recovery. Geographic isolation from treatment options disproportionately affects rural populations and contributes to disparities in substance use disorder outcomes.
substance_near_10_median.long_name: Substance abuse treatment availability by median drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
substance_near_10_median.short_description: Median driving time in minutes to the 10 nearest substance abuse treatment facilities.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

substance_near_10_median.provenance: Facility locations downloaded from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=SA). Drive times computed using pre-computed block-group-to-block-group OSRM travel time matrices. For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest facilities by drive time are identified and the median travel time is reported. Aggregated to tract and county via simple mean.
substance_near_10_median.statement: The median drive time to the ten closest substance abuse treatment facilities to {features.name} is {value}.

substance_near_10_mean.long_description: Mean drive time in minutes to the ten closest substance abuse treatment facilities. This measure captures the average geographic accessibility of treatment options, providing a complementary view to the median by being sensitive to outlier facilities that may be significantly farther away.
substance_near_10_mean.long_name: Substance abuse treatment availability by mean drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
substance_near_10_mean.short_description: Mean driving time in minutes to the 10 nearest substance abuse treatment facilities.
substance_near_10_mean.short_name: Substance abuse treatment availability (mean drive time)

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

substance_near_10_mean.provenance: Facility locations downloaded from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=SA). Drive times computed using pre-computed block-group-to-block-group OSRM travel time matrices. For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest facilities by drive time are identified and the mean travel time is reported. Aggregated to tract and county via simple mean.
substance_near_10_mean.statement: The mean drive time to the ten closest substance abuse treatment facilities to {features.name} is {value}.

substance_cnt.long_description: Count of substance abuse treatment facilities located within each geographic area. This measure indicates the raw availability of treatment options. Areas with no or very few facilities may represent service deserts where residents must travel long distances to access substance abuse treatment.
substance_cnt.long_name: Availability of substance abuse treatment by facility count
substance_cnt.short_description: Number of substance abuse treatment facilities in the area.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

substance_cnt.provenance: Facility locations downloaded from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=SA). Each facility is assigned to the nearest block group centroid via haversine distance. Counts are summed when aggregating to tract, county, and health district levels.
substance_cnt.statement: There are {value} substance abuse treatment facilities in {features.name}.

substance_2sfca.long_description: Substance abuse treatment accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method. This spatial access index accounts for both the supply of treatment facilities and the demand from the surrounding population, identifying areas where high population density relative to facility supply creates access bottlenecks.
substance_2sfca.long_name: Substance abuse treatment geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
substance_2sfca.short_description: Spatial access score for substance abuse treatment using the 2SFCA method.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

substance_2sfca.provenance: Computed using the 2-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method (Luo, 2004) with a 30-minute drive time threshold. Provider supply from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=SA), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand from ACS B01001_001 total population at block group level. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
substance_2sfca.statement: The substance abuse treatment accessibility score for {features.name} is {value}.

substance_e2sfca.long_description: Substance abuse treatment accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method. This method improves on the basic 2SFCA by applying stepped distance decay weights, so nearer facilities contribute more to the access score than distant ones within the catchment area.
substance_e2sfca.long_name: Substance abuse treatment geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
substance_e2sfca.short_description: Spatial access score for substance abuse treatment using the E2SFCA method with distance decay.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

substance_e2sfca.provenance: Computed using the enhanced 2-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method (Luo & Qi, 2009) with stepped weights: 10min=0.962, 20min=0.704, 30min=0.377, 60min=0.042. Provider supply from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=SA), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand from ACS B01001_001. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
substance_e2sfca.statement: The substance abuse treatment accessibility score (E2SFCA) for {features.name} is {value}.

substance_3sfca.long_description: Substance abuse treatment accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance decay. This is the most sophisticated FCA variant, normalizing competition weights so that the total demand allocated from each consumer sums to one, producing more realistic access estimates in areas with overlapping catchments.
substance_3sfca.long_name: Substance abuse treatment geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
substance_3sfca.short_description: Spatial access score for substance abuse treatment using the 3SFCA method with Gaussian decay.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

substance_3sfca.provenance: Computed using the 3-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method (Wan et al., 2012) with Gaussian distance decay (scale=20 minutes) and normalized competition weights. Provider supply from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=SA), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand from ACS B01001_001. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
substance_3sfca.statement: The substance abuse treatment accessibility score (3SFCA) for {features.name} is {value}.

ems_cnt.long_description: Count of emergency medical services (EMS) stations located within each geographic unit. EMS station availability varies widely between urban and rural areas, and lower station counts can indicate longer response times during medical emergencies, potentially affecting survival outcomes for time-sensitive conditions like cardiac arrest and stroke.
ems_cnt.long_name: Availability of emergency medical services by count
ems_cnt.short_description: Number of EMS stations within each geographic area.
ems_cnt.provenance: Station locations from HIFLD Emergency Medical Service Stations dataset (2021 snapshot). Each station counted as capacity=1. Stations snapped to nearest 2020 Census block group centroid using haversine distance. Counts summed from block group to tract and county levels.
ems_cnt.statement: There are {value} emergency medical services facilities in {features.name}.

ems_2sfca.long_description: EMS accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method with a 30-minute travel time threshold. This method computes provider-to-population ratios within catchment areas to identify communities with limited emergency medical service coverage. Areas with lower scores face longer expected response times, which is critical for time-sensitive emergencies.
ems_2sfca.long_name: Emergency medical services geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
ems_2sfca.short_description: EMS accessibility index using two-step floating catchment area analysis with a 30-minute threshold.
ems_2sfca.provenance: Computed using 2SFCA methodology (Luo & Wang, 2003; Luo, 2004) with 30-minute binary travel time threshold. Provider capacity: 1 per HIFLD EMS station (2021). Consumer demand: ACS 2021 total population (B01001_001) at block group level. Travel times: pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG drive times. Values expressed per 1,000 population.
ems_2sfca.statement: The 2SFCA emergency medical services availability for {features.name} is {value}.

ems_e2sfca.long_description: EMS accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method with stepped distance-decay weights. Unlike the basic 2SFCA, this method applies decreasing weights at 10, 20, 30, and 60 minute travel time zones, reflecting the reality that people are less likely to use facilities that are farther away. Lower scores indicate communities with reduced EMS accessibility.
ems_e2sfca.long_name: Emergency medical services geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
ems_e2sfca.short_description: EMS accessibility index using enhanced two-step floating catchment area analysis with distance-decay weights.
ems_e2sfca.provenance: Computed using E2SFCA methodology (Luo & Qi, 2009) with stepped weights: 10 min (0.962), 20 min (0.704), 30 min (0.377), 60 min (0.042). Provider capacity: 1 per HIFLD EMS station (2021). Consumer demand: ACS 2021 total population (B01001_001) at block group level. Travel times: pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG drive times. Values expressed per 1,000 population.
ems_e2sfca.statement: The E2SFCA emergency medical services availability for {features.name} is {value}.

ems_3sfca.long_description: EMS accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance-decay weighting. This is the most sophisticated FCA variant, incorporating both supply-side and demand-side competition effects with a smooth Gaussian decay function. It provides the most nuanced measure of spatial accessibility to emergency medical services.
ems_3sfca.long_name: Emergency medical services geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
ems_3sfca.short_description: EMS accessibility index using three-step floating catchment area analysis with Gaussian weighting.
ems_3sfca.provenance: Computed using 3SFCA methodology (Wan et al., 2012; Saxon et al., 2020) with Gaussian distance decay (scale=20 minutes). Provider capacity: 1 per HIFLD EMS station (2021). Consumer demand: ACS 2021 total population (B01001_001) at block group level. Travel times: pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG drive times. Normalized weights. Values expressed per 1,000 population.
ems_3sfca.statement: The emergency medical services availability for {features.name} is {value}.

ems_near_10_mean.long_description: Mean drive time in minutes to the ten closest emergency medical services stations. This measure captures the average travel burden to multiple nearby EMS facilities, providing a more robust accessibility indicator than nearest-facility distance alone. Higher values indicate areas with fewer nearby EMS resources, where emergency response times may be longer.
ems_near_10_mean.long_name: Emergency medical services availability by mean drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
ems_near_10_mean.short_description: Average drive time in minutes to the ten nearest EMS stations.
ems_near_10_mean.provenance: For each block group, the 10 shortest OSRM drive times to block groups containing HIFLD EMS stations (2021) are identified and averaged. Self-pairs (BGs containing a station) are assigned 0 minutes. Aggregated to tract and county by simple mean of constituent block groups.
ems_near_10_mean.statement: The mean drive time to the ten closest emergency medical service facilities to {features.name} is {value}.

ems_near_10_median.long_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten closest emergency medical services stations. The median is less sensitive to outliers than the mean, providing a robust central estimate of travel time to nearby EMS facilities. Communities with high median drive times have fewer accessible emergency services, which may affect outcomes for time-critical medical events.
ems_near_10_median.long_name: Emergency medical services availability by median drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
ems_near_10_median.short_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten nearest EMS stations.
ems_near_10_median.provenance: For each block group, the 10 shortest OSRM drive times to block groups containing HIFLD EMS stations (2021) are identified and the median is taken. Self-pairs (BGs containing a station) are assigned 0 minutes. Aggregated to tract and county by simple mean of constituent block groups.
ems_near_10_median.statement: The median drive time to the ten closest emergency medical service facilities to {features.name} is {value}.

hosp_near_10_median.long_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten nearest hospitals from each block group centroid. Shorter drive times indicate better geographic proximity to hospital care, which is critical for emergency medical situations where every minute matters. Areas with long median drive times may face barriers to timely treatment for strokes, heart attacks, and traumatic injuries.
hosp_near_10_median.long_name: Hospital availability by median drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
hosp_near_10_median.short_description: Median travel time in minutes to the 10 nearest hospitals, measuring geographic proximity to hospital care.
hosp_near_10_median.provenance: Hospital facility locations from CMS Hospital Compare (2015-2025), filtered to VA/DC/MD non-psychiatric hospitals. Geocoded via HIFLD (primary) and Census Geocoder (fallback). Drive times computed using OSRM-based block-group-to-block-group travel time matrix. Each block group's median is taken over travel times to the 10 nearest hospital block groups.

sources.name: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services

hosp_near_10_median.statement: The median drive time to the ten closest hospitals to {features.name} is {value}.

hosp_near_10_mean.long_description: Mean drive time in minutes to the ten nearest hospitals from each block group centroid. This measure complements the median by capturing the overall distribution of hospital proximity. Higher mean times suggest that even when the closest hospital is accessible, the broader hospital network may be sparse, limiting patient choice and surge capacity.
hosp_near_10_mean.long_name: Hospital availability by mean drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
hosp_near_10_mean.short_description: Average travel time in minutes to the 10 nearest hospitals.
hosp_near_10_mean.provenance: Hospital facility locations from CMS Hospital Compare (2015-2025), filtered to VA/DC/MD non-psychiatric hospitals. Geocoded via HIFLD (primary) and Census Geocoder (fallback). Drive times computed using OSRM block-group-to-block-group travel time matrix. Each block group's mean is taken over travel times to the 10 nearest hospital block groups.

sources.name: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services

hosp_near_10_mean.statement: The mean drive time to the ten closest hospitals to {features.name} is {value}.

hosp_3sfca.long_description: Hospital accessibility score based on the 3-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance decay. This model accounts for both the supply of hospitals relative to population demand and the distance residents must travel. Higher scores indicate areas with better hospital accessibility relative to need, while lower scores highlight potential gaps in hospital coverage.
hosp_3sfca.long_name: Hospital geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
hosp_3sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index measuring hospital availability relative to population, weighted by Gaussian distance decay.
hosp_3sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the 3-step floating catchment area methodology (Wan et al., 2012) with Gaussian weights (scale=20 minutes). Provider supply: CMS Hospital Compare facility locations (2015-2025), each hospital capacity=1. Consumer demand: ACS B01001_001 total population at block group level. Travel cost: OSRM-based block-group-to-block-group driving times. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.

sources.name: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services

hosp_3sfca.statement: The hospital geographic availability score for {features.name} is {value}.

hosp_2sfca.long_description: Hospital accessibility score based on the 2-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method with a 30-minute travel time threshold. This binary-weight model identifies areas where hospital capacity is insufficient relative to the surrounding population. It is simpler than stepped or Gaussian variants but provides an intuitive measure of hospital accessibility within a reasonable driving distance.
hosp_2sfca.long_name: Hospital geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
hosp_2sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index using binary-weight 2SFCA with a 30-minute threshold.
hosp_2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the 2-step floating catchment area methodology (Luo & Wang, 2003) with a 30-minute binary threshold. Provider supply: CMS Hospital Compare (2015-2025), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand: ACS B01001_001 total population at block group level. Travel cost: OSRM block-group-to-block-group driving times. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.

sources.name: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services

hosp_2sfca.statement: The hospital 2SFCA availability score for {features.name} is {value}.

hosp_e2sfca.long_description: Hospital accessibility score based on the enhanced 2-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method with stepped distance weights. This variant improves on the standard 2SFCA by applying decreasing weights at 10, 20, 30, and 60-minute travel time bands, better reflecting the real-world pattern where people are less likely to travel to distant hospitals.
hosp_e2sfca.long_name: Hospital geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
hosp_e2sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index using E2SFCA with stepped distance decay weights.
hosp_e2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the enhanced 2-step floating catchment area methodology (Luo & Qi, 2009) with stepped weights: 10min=0.962, 20min=0.704, 30min=0.377, 60min=0.042. Provider supply: CMS Hospital Compare (2015-2025), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand: ACS B01001_001 total population at block group level. Travel cost: OSRM block-group-to-block-group driving times. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.

sources.name: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services

hosp_e2sfca.statement: The hospital E2SFCA availability score for {features.name} is {value}.

hosp_cnt.long_description: Count of non-psychiatric hospitals located within each geographic area. This measure captures the raw supply of hospital facilities, providing a simple indicator of health infrastructure density. Areas with zero or very few hospitals may rely heavily on facilities in neighboring areas, increasing travel burden for residents.
hosp_cnt.short_description: Number of non-psychiatric hospitals (Acute Care, Critical Access, Children's) in each area.
hosp_cnt.provenance: Facility count from CMS Hospital Compare (2015-2025), filtered to VA/DC/MD. Excludes psychiatric hospitals. Each facility counts as 1 regardless of size or bed count. Geocoded via HIFLD (primary) and Census Geocoder (fallback), snapped to nearest block group centroid.

sources.name: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services

mental_near_10_median.long_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten closest mental health treatment facilities. Geographic barriers to mental health care are a major contributor to unmet treatment needs, particularly in rural areas. Longer drive times reduce the likelihood of initiating and sustaining treatment, exacerbating disparities in mental health outcomes.
mental_near_10_median.long_name: Mental health services availability by median drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
mental_near_10_median.short_description: Median driving time in minutes to the 10 nearest mental health treatment facilities.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

mental_near_10_median.provenance: Facility locations downloaded from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=MH). Drive times computed using pre-computed block-group-to-block-group OSRM travel time matrices. For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest facilities by drive time are identified and the median travel time is reported. Aggregated to tract and county via simple mean.
mental_near_10_median.statement: The median drive time to the ten closest mental health facilities to {features.name} is {value}.

mental_near_10_mean.long_description: Mean drive time in minutes to the ten closest mental health treatment facilities. This measure captures the average geographic accessibility of mental health services, providing a complementary view to the median by being sensitive to outlier facilities that may be significantly farther away.
mental_near_10_mean.long_name: Mental health services availability by mean drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
mental_near_10_mean.short_description: Mean driving time in minutes to the 10 nearest mental health treatment facilities.
mental_near_10_mean.short_name: Mental health services availability (mean drive time)

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

mental_near_10_mean.provenance: Facility locations downloaded from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=MH). Drive times computed using pre-computed block-group-to-block-group OSRM travel time matrices. For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest facilities by drive time are identified and the mean travel time is reported. Aggregated to tract and county via simple mean.
mental_near_10_mean.statement: The mean drive time to the ten closest mental health facilities to {features.name} is {value}.

mental_cnt.long_description: Count of mental health treatment facilities located within each geographic area. This measure indicates the raw availability of mental health services. Areas with no or very few facilities may represent service deserts where residents must travel long distances to access mental health care.
mental_cnt.long_name: Availability of mental health services by facility count
mental_cnt.short_description: Number of mental health treatment facilities in the area.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

mental_cnt.provenance: Facility locations downloaded from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=MH). Each facility is assigned to the nearest block group centroid via haversine distance. Counts are summed when aggregating to tract, county, and health district levels.
mental_cnt.statement: There are {value} mental health facilities in {features.name}.

mental_2sfca.long_description: Mental health service accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method. This spatial access index accounts for both the supply of treatment facilities and the demand from the surrounding population, identifying areas where high population density relative to facility supply creates access bottlenecks.
mental_2sfca.long_name: Mental health services geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
mental_2sfca.short_description: Spatial access score for mental health services using the 2SFCA method.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

mental_2sfca.provenance: Computed using the 2-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method (Luo, 2004) with a 30-minute drive time threshold. Provider supply from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=MH), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand from ACS B01001_001 total population at block group level. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
mental_2sfca.statement: The mental health services accessibility score for {features.name} is {value}.

mental_e2sfca.long_description: Mental health service accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method. This method improves on the basic 2SFCA by applying stepped distance decay weights, so nearer facilities contribute more to the access score than distant ones within the catchment area.
mental_e2sfca.long_name: Mental health services geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
mental_e2sfca.short_description: Spatial access score for mental health services using the E2SFCA method with distance decay.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

mental_e2sfca.provenance: Computed using the enhanced 2-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method (Luo & Qi, 2009) with stepped weights: 10min=0.962, 20min=0.704, 30min=0.377, 60min=0.042. Provider supply from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=MH), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand from ACS B01001_001. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
mental_e2sfca.statement: The mental health services accessibility score (E2SFCA) for {features.name} is {value}.

mental_3sfca.long_description: Mental health service accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance decay. This is the most sophisticated FCA variant, normalizing competition weights so that the total demand allocated from each consumer sums to one, producing more realistic access estimates in areas with overlapping catchments.
mental_3sfca.long_name: Mental health services geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
mental_3sfca.short_description: Spatial access score for mental health services using the 3SFCA method with Gaussian decay.

sources.name: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

mental_3sfca.provenance: Computed using the 3-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method (Wan et al., 2012) with Gaussian distance decay (scale=20 minutes) and normalized competition weights. Provider supply from SAMHSA findtreatment.gov API (sType=MH), capacity=1 per facility. Consumer demand from ACS B01001_001. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
mental_3sfca.statement: The mental health services accessibility score (3SFCA) for {features.name} is {value}.

obgyn_cnt.long_description: Count of OB-GYN physicians per block group, derived from CMS Doctors and Clinicians enrollment data. Areas with few or no OB-GYN providers face potential gaps in reproductive and maternal health care, which can lead to delayed prenatal visits and worse birth outcomes.
obgyn_cnt.short_description: Number of OB-GYN physicians located in each geographic area.
obgyn_cnt.provenance: Provider counts from CMS Doctors and Clinicians national downloadable file, filtered to Obstetrics/Gynecology specialty (primary or secondary) with MD/DO credentials in VA, DC, and MD. Addresses geocoded via Census Geocoder and snapped to nearest 2020 block group centroid. Counts represent unique NPIs per block group.

obgyn_near_10_mean.long_description: Average travel time in minutes to the nearest 10 OB-GYN provider locations from each block group. This measure captures geographic barriers to reproductive health care: longer travel times are associated with fewer prenatal visits, delayed emergency obstetric care, and higher rates of adverse maternal outcomes.
obgyn_near_10_mean.long_name: Mean travel time to nearest 10 OB-GYN locations
obgyn_near_10_mean.short_description: Mean driving time in minutes to the 10 closest OB-GYN provider locations.
obgyn_near_10_mean.provenance: Travel times from pre-computed OSRM block-group-to-block-group driving time matrix. For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest provider block groups (by travel time) are identified and the mean travel time is computed. Provider locations from CMS Doctors and Clinicians, filtered to Obstetrics/Gynecology specialty.
obgyn_near_10_mean.statement: The mean travel time to the nearest 10 OB-GYN locations from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

obgyn_near_10_median.long_description: Median travel time in minutes to the nearest 10 OB-GYN provider locations from each block group. The median is less sensitive to outlier locations than the mean, providing a robust indicator of typical geographic access to reproductive health services.
obgyn_near_10_median.long_name: Median travel time to nearest 10 OB-GYN locations
obgyn_near_10_median.short_description: Median driving time in minutes to the 10 closest OB-GYN provider locations.
obgyn_near_10_median.provenance: Travel times from pre-computed OSRM block-group-to-block-group driving time matrix. For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest provider block groups (by travel time) are identified and the median travel time is computed. Provider locations from CMS Doctors and Clinicians, filtered to Obstetrics/Gynecology specialty.
obgyn_near_10_median.statement: The median travel time to the nearest 10 OB-GYN locations from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

obgyn_2sfca.long_description: OB-GYN care accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method with a 30-minute travel threshold. This index quantifies geographic access to reproductive health services by computing provider-to-population ratios within overlapping service areas. Lower scores indicate areas where the female population age 15+ faces greater competition for limited OB-GYN capacity.
obgyn_2sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index for OB-GYN services using the two-step floating catchment area method.
obgyn_2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the 2SFCA methodology (Luo, 2004) with a 30-minute travel time threshold. Provider capacity = unique NPI count per location from CMS Doctors and Clinicians (Obstetrics/Gynecology). Consumer demand = female population age 15+ from ACS table B01001 (variables B01001_030 through B01001_049). Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrix. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.

obgyn_e2sfca.long_description: OB-GYN care accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method with stepped distance-decay weights. Unlike the basic 2SFCA, E2SFCA applies decreasing weights at 10, 20, 30, and 60-minute travel bands, reflecting the reality that patients are less likely to use providers farther away. This produces a more realistic picture of effective access to reproductive health care.
obgyn_e2sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index for OB-GYN services using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area method with distance-decay weights.
obgyn_e2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the E2SFCA methodology (Luo & Qi, 2009) with stepped weights: 10 min (0.962), 20 min (0.704), 30 min (0.377), 60 min (0.042). Provider capacity = unique NPI count per location from CMS Doctors and Clinicians (Obstetrics/Gynecology). Consumer demand = female population age 15+ from ACS table B01001 (variables B01001_030 through B01001_049). Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrix. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.

obgyn_3sfca.long_description: OB-GYN care accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance decay. The 3SFCA adds a selection-weight step that accounts for the competitive effect among nearby providers, yielding an accessibility score that reflects both spatial proximity and choice behavior in reproductive health service utilization.
obgyn_3sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index for OB-GYN services using the three-step floating catchment area method with Gaussian decay.
obgyn_3sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the 3SFCA methodology (Wan et al., 2012) with Gaussian distance decay (scale=20). Provider capacity = unique NPI count per location from CMS Doctors and Clinicians (Obstetrics/Gynecology). Consumer demand = female population age 15+ from ACS table B01001 (variables B01001_030 through B01001_049). Travel times from pre-computed OSRM BG-to-BG matrix. Scores normalized by selection weights.

peds_cnt.long_description: Count of pediatric medicine physicians located within each geographic area. Pediatricians are identified from the CMS Doctors and Clinicians dataset by filtering to providers with a PEDIATRIC MEDICINE specialty designation and MD/DO credentials. Higher counts indicate greater local availability of pediatric care providers, though counts alone do not account for population demand or geographic accessibility barriers.
peds_cnt.short_description: Number of pediatric medicine physicians (MD/DO) from CMS Doctors and Clinicians data within each area.
peds_cnt.provenance: Provider counts derived from CMS Doctors and Clinicians dataset (2018-2025), filtered to PEDIATRIC MEDICINE specialty with MD/DO credentials in VA/DC/MD. Addresses geocoded via Census Geocoder API and snapped to nearest 2020 block group centroid. Counts summed from block group to tract, county, and health district.

peds_near_10_mean.long_description: Average travel time in minutes to the nearest 10 pediatric medicine physicians from each block group centroid. This measure captures geographic proximity to pediatric care, with higher values indicating communities where families must travel farther to access multiple pediatric providers. Areas with long travel times to pediatricians may face barriers to routine well-child visits, vaccinations, and acute care for children.
peds_near_10_mean.long_name: Mean travel time to nearest 10 pediatricians
peds_near_10_mean.short_description: Mean driving time in minutes from block group centroid to the 10 nearest pediatric medicine physicians.
peds_near_10_mean.short_name: Mean travel time to nearest 10 pediatricians
peds_near_10_mean.provenance: Travel times from pre-computed OSRM block-group-to-block-group driving time matrices (2020 boundaries). For each block group, the 10 nearest pediatrician locations (by travel time) are identified and their mean travel time computed. Aggregated to tract/county/HD via simple mean across constituent block groups.
peds_near_10_mean.statement: The mean travel time to the nearest 10 pediatricians from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

peds_near_10_median.long_description: Median travel time in minutes to the nearest 10 pediatric medicine physicians from each block group centroid. The median is more robust to outliers than the mean, providing a better sense of typical accessibility. Communities with high median travel times face systematic barriers to pediatric care access that may result in delayed treatment and lower utilization of preventive services for children.
peds_near_10_median.long_name: Median travel time to nearest 10 pediatricians
peds_near_10_median.short_description: Median driving time in minutes from block group centroid to the 10 nearest pediatric medicine physicians.
peds_near_10_median.short_name: Median travel time to nearest 10 pediatricians
peds_near_10_median.provenance: Travel times from pre-computed OSRM block-group-to-block-group driving time matrices (2020 boundaries). For each block group, the 10 nearest pediatrician locations (by travel time) are identified and their median travel time computed. Aggregated to tract/county/HD via simple mean of block group medians.
peds_near_10_median.statement: The median travel time to the nearest 10 pediatricians from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

peds_2sfca.long_description: Pediatric care accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method. This index accounts for both the supply of pediatricians and the demand from the population aged 0-17, weighted by travel time within a 30-minute catchment. Higher values indicate better spatial access to pediatric care relative to the child population, while lower values suggest potential shortages that could affect timely access to well-child visits and acute care.
peds_2sfca.long_name: Pediatric care geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
peds_2sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index for pediatric physicians per 1,000 children aged 0-17, using 2-step floating catchment area method.
peds_2sfca.provenance: Two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) with 30-minute travel time threshold. Supply: CMS Doctors and Clinicians PEDIATRIC MEDICINE providers (MD/DO) in VA/DC/MD, geocoded and snapped to 2020 block group centroids. Demand: ACS B01001 population ages 0-17 (variables B01001_003-006, B01001_027-030) at block group level. Travel costs from OSRM driving time matrices. Values expressed per 1,000 population. Aggregated to tract/county via population-weighted mean.
peds_2sfca.statement: The pediatric care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

peds_e2sfca.long_description: Pediatric care accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method. Unlike the basic 2SFCA, this variant applies stepped distance decay weights (10 min: 0.962, 20 min: 0.704, 30 min: 0.377, 60 min: 0.042), better reflecting how families' willingness to travel for pediatric care decreases with distance. Higher values indicate greater spatial accessibility to pediatric physicians relative to child population demand.
peds_e2sfca.long_name: Pediatric care geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
peds_e2sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index for pediatric physicians per 1,000 children aged 0-17, using enhanced 2-step floating catchment area method with distance decay.
peds_e2sfca.provenance: Enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) with stepped distance decay weights: 10 min=0.962, 20 min=0.704, 30 min=0.377, 60 min=0.042. Supply: CMS Doctors and Clinicians PEDIATRIC MEDICINE providers. Demand: ACS B01001 population ages 0-17. Travel costs from OSRM driving time matrices. Values per 1,000 population. Aggregated to tract/county via population-weighted mean.
peds_e2sfca.statement: The pediatric care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

peds_3sfca.long_description: Pediatric care accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance decay. This is the most sophisticated FCA variant, normalizing competition weights so that each consumer's demand is distributed proportionally across reachable providers. It provides the most realistic estimate of pediatric care accessibility by accounting for provider competition and distance sensitivity simultaneously.
peds_3sfca.long_name: Pediatric care geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
peds_3sfca.short_description: Spatial accessibility index for pediatric physicians per 1,000 children aged 0-17, using 3-step floating catchment area method with Gaussian decay.
peds_3sfca.provenance: Three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) with Gaussian distance decay (scale=20) and normalized competition weights. Supply: CMS Doctors and Clinicians PEDIATRIC MEDICINE providers. Demand: ACS B01001 population ages 0-17. Travel costs from OSRM driving time matrices. Values per 1,000 population. Aggregated to tract/county via population-weighted mean.
peds_3sfca.statement: The pediatric care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

primcare_cnt.long_description: Count of primary care physicians accessible within each geography's floating catchment area. Primary care physicians include family practice, family medicine, and general practice specialties enrolled in Medicare. Areas with low provider counts may face longer wait times and reduced access to preventive care, contributing to worse health outcomes over time.
primcare_cnt.short_description: Number of Medicare-enrolled primary care physicians accessible within a geography's catchment area.
primcare_cnt.provenance: Provider locations from CMS Doctors and Clinicians dataset (2022), filtered to Family Practice, Family Medicine, and General Practice specialties in VA, DC, and MD. Addresses geocoded to latitude/longitude. Provider capacity estimated as 1.0 per unique NPI. Counts reflect the number of providers within the floating catchment area of each block group, aggregated to higher geographies by population-weighted mean.
primcare_cnt.statement: There are {value} primary care physicians in {features.name}.

primcare_2sfca.long_description: Primary care accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method, which computes physician-to-population ratios weighted by travel time within catchment areas. Higher values indicate greater spatial access to primary care. Areas with low scores may face physician shortages, leading to delayed preventive care and higher emergency department utilization.
primcare_2sfca.long_name: Primary care geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
primcare_2sfca.short_description: Spatial access index for primary care physicians based on the 2SFCA method using travel-time-weighted supply-to-demand ratios.
primcare_2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method (Luo, 2004). Provider locations from CMS Doctors and Clinicians (2022), filtered to Family Practice/Family Medicine/General Practice in VA/DC/MD. Demand from ACS 2020 block group population. Travel times from OSRM block-group-to-block-group matrices. Catchment threshold: 30 minutes driving.
primcare_2sfca.statement: The primary care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

primcare_e2sfca.long_description: Primary care accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method, which improves on 2SFCA by applying distance-decay weights within catchment zones. This produces a more realistic accessibility measure since patients are more likely to visit closer providers. Low scores indicate areas where residents face significant barriers to reaching primary care.
primcare_e2sfca.long_name: Primary care geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
primcare_e2sfca.short_description: Spatial access index for primary care physicians based on the E2SFCA method with distance-decay weighting within catchment zones.
primcare_e2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method (Luo & Qi, 2009). Provider locations from CMS Doctors and Clinicians (2022), filtered to Family Practice/Family Medicine/General Practice in VA/DC/MD. Demand from ACS 2020 block group population. Travel times from OSRM block-group-to-block-group matrices. Catchment threshold: 30 minutes driving with three distance-decay zones.
primcare_e2sfca.statement: The primary care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

primcare_3sfca.long_description: Primary care accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method, which extends E2SFCA by adding a selection-weight step that accounts for competition among providers. This method better captures how patients choose among multiple nearby providers. Low scores indicate areas with both fewer providers and higher competition for available appointments.
primcare_3sfca.long_name: Primary care geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
primcare_3sfca.short_description: Spatial access index for primary care physicians based on the 3SFCA method with provider competition weighting.
primcare_3sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method (Wan et al., 2012). Provider locations from CMS Doctors and Clinicians (2022), filtered to Family Practice/Family Medicine/General Practice in VA/DC/MD. Demand from ACS 2020 block group population. Travel times from OSRM block-group-to-block-group matrices. Catchment threshold: 30 minutes driving with distance-decay and provider-competition weights.
primcare_3sfca.statement: The primary care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

primcare_near_10_mean.long_description: Mean drive time in minutes to the ten closest primary care physician locations. This measure captures the average effort required to reach nearby primary care options. Areas with high mean drive times have fewer geographically proximate providers, which can reduce the likelihood of regular preventive care visits.
primcare_near_10_mean.long_name: Primary care availability by mean drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
primcare_near_10_mean.short_description: Average drive time in minutes from a block group centroid to the ten nearest primary care physician locations.
primcare_near_10_mean.provenance: Drive times computed using OSRM block-group-to-block-group travel time matrices. For each block group, the ten closest primary care physician locations (CMS Doctors and Clinicians, 2022) are identified and the mean travel time is reported. Aggregated to higher geographies by population-weighted mean.
primcare_near_10_mean.statement: The mean drive time to the ten closest primary care physicians from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

primcare_near_10_median.long_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten closest primary care physician locations. The median is less sensitive to outlier distances than the mean, providing a robust measure of typical travel burden. High median drive times indicate areas where even the most accessible providers require substantial travel, a barrier to routine primary care utilization.
primcare_near_10_median.long_name: Primary care availability by median drive time to the ten closest facilities in minutes
primcare_near_10_median.short_description: Median drive time in minutes from a block group centroid to the ten nearest primary care physician locations.
primcare_near_10_median.provenance: Drive times computed using OSRM block-group-to-block-group travel time matrices. For each block group, the ten closest primary care physician locations (CMS Doctors and Clinicians, 2022) are identified and the median travel time is reported. Aggregated to higher geographies by population-weighted mean.
primcare_near_10_median.statement: The median drive time to the ten closest primary care physicians from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

urgent_cnt.long_description: Count of urgent care facilities located within each geographic area, based on geocoded locations from NPPES NPI Registry (taxonomy 261QU0200X). Areas with zero or very few urgent care centers may indicate gaps in access to acute non-emergency medical services, forcing residents to rely on more distant or more costly emergency departments.
urgent_cnt.long_name: Availability of urgent care by count
urgent_cnt.short_description: Number of urgent care facilities per geographic area from NPPES NPI Registry (taxonomy 261QU0200X).
urgent_cnt.provenance: Facility locations from NPPES NPI Registry, filtered to taxonomy code 261QU0200X (Urgent Care Clinic/Center) in VA/DC/MD. Addresses geocoded via Census Geocoder API and snapped to nearest 2020 Census block group centroid. Each facility treated as capacity=1. Counts aggregated from block group to tract, county, and health district levels.
urgent_cnt.statement: There are {value} urgent care facilities in {features.name}.

urgent_2sfca.long_description: Urgent care accessibility measured using the two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method with a 30-minute travel time threshold. This index captures both the supply of urgent care facilities and the demand from surrounding populations, identifying areas where residents may face barriers to timely non-emergency medical care.
urgent_2sfca.long_name: Urgent care geographic availability (2-step floating catchment areas)
urgent_2sfca.short_description: Urgent-care-to-population accessibility ratio using the 2SFCA method with a 30-minute catchment.
urgent_2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the 2-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) methodology (Luo, 2004). Facility locations from NPPES NPI Registry (taxonomy 261QU0200X), each with capacity=1, snapped to block group centroids. Consumer population from ACS 5-year table B01001_001 at block group level for VA, MD, and DC. Travel times from pre-computed OSRM block-group-to-block-group matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.
urgent_2sfca.statement: The urgent care availability for {features.name} is {value}.

urgent_e2sfca.long_description: Urgent care accessibility measured using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method, which applies distance-decay weights within stepped travel time zones (10, 20, 30, and 60 minutes). Unlike the binary 2SFCA, E2SFCA recognizes that closer facilities are more accessible, producing a more realistic picture of urgent care availability across the region.
urgent_e2sfca.long_name: Urgent care geographic availability (enhanced 2-step floating catchment areas)
urgent_e2sfca.short_description: Urgent-care-to-population accessibility ratio using the E2SFCA method with stepped distance-decay weights.
urgent_e2sfca.provenance: Spatial access scores computed using the enhanced 2-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) methodology (Luo & Qi, 2009). Stepped weights: 10 min=0.962, 20 min=0.704, 30 min=0.377, 60 min=0.042. Facility locations from NPPES NPI Registry (taxonomy 261QU0200X), consumer population from ACS 5-year B01001_001 at block group level. Travel times from OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. Scores expressed per 1,000 population.

urgent_3sfca.long_description: Urgent care accessibility measured using the three-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method with Gaussian distance decay. This variant accounts for competition among consumers for facility capacity and applies a smooth Gaussian weight function, providing the most nuanced estimate of spatial access to urgent care services.
urgent_3sfca.long_name: Urgent care geographic availability (3-step floating catchment areas)
urgent_3sfca.short_description: Urgent-care-to-population accessibility ratio using the 3SFCA method with Gaussian distance decay.

Facility locations from NPPES NPI Registry (taxonomy 261QU0200X), consumer population from ACS 5-year B01001_001 at block group level. Travel times from OSRM BG-to-BG matrices. urgent_near_10_mean.long_description: Mean drive time in minutes to the ten nearest urgent care facilities from each block group. This measure captures the travel burden residents face when seeking non-emergency acute care, with higher values indicating greater geographic isolation from urgent care services.
urgent_near_10_mean.long_name: Mean drive time to ten nearest urgent care facilities
urgent_near_10_mean.short_description: Average driving time in minutes to the ten closest urgent care facilities.
urgent_near_10_mean.short_name: Mean drive time to nearest urgent care

For each consumer block group, the 10 nearest provider block groups (by travel time) are identified and the mean travel time computed. Facility locations from NPPES NPI Registry (taxonomy 261QU0200X). Aggregated to tract and county by simple mean.
urgent_near_10_mean.statement: The mean drive time to the ten nearest urgent care facilities from {features.name} is {value} minutes.

urgent_near_10_median.long_description: Median drive time in minutes to the ten nearest urgent care facilities from each block group. The median is less sensitive to outlier travel times than the mean, providing a robust measure of typical travel burden for accessing non-emergency acute care.
urgent_near_10_median.long_name: Median drive time to ten nearest urgent care facilities
urgent_near_10_median.short_description: Median driving time in minutes to the ten closest urgent care facilities.
urgent_near_10_median.short_name: Median drive time to nearest urgent care
