economic_opportunity_profile_geo20.long_description: PCA-derived profile score measuring economic opportunities within a community. Computed from three min-max normalized indicators: employment access index, income inequality (Gini, inverted), and labor force participation rate. Weights are from the VDH HOI PCA weight matrix. Higher values indicate greater economic opportunity.
economic_opportunity_profile_geo20.short_description: PCA-weighted composite of employment access, income inequality, and labor force participation.
economic_opportunity_profile_geo20.provenance: Reproduced from VDH HOI methodology using independently computed sub-indicators from Census ACS, LODES, EPA EJScreen, USDA FARA, CMS PUF, Vera Institute, and other sources. Each indicator is min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, then combined via VDH's PCA weight matrix to produce profile scores.
economic_opportunity_profile_geo20.statement: The economic opportunity profile score in {features.name} is {value}.

consumer_opportunity_profile_geo20.long_description: PCA-derived profile score measuring consumer resources available within a community. Computed from four min-max normalized indicators: H+T affordability index (inverted), average years of schooling, food access percentage (inverted), and material deprivation index (inverted). Weights are from the VDH HOI PCA weight matrix. Higher values indicate greater consumer opportunity.
consumer_opportunity_profile_geo20.short_description: PCA-weighted composite of affordability, education, food access, and material deprivation.
consumer_opportunity_profile_geo20.provenance: Reproduced from VDH HOI methodology using independently computed sub-indicators from Census ACS, LODES, EPA EJScreen, USDA FARA, CMS PUF, Vera Institute, and other sources. Each indicator is min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, then combined via VDH's PCA weight matrix to produce profile scores.
consumer_opportunity_profile_geo20.statement: The consumer opportunity profile score in {features.name} is {value}.

social_impact_profile_geo20.long_description: PCA-derived profile score measuring disparate access to health services and social cohesion within a community. Computed from three min-max normalized indicators: access to care index, segregation index (inverted), and incarceration rate (inverted). Weights are from the VDH HOI PCA weight matrix. Higher values indicate lower social impact burden.
social_impact_profile_geo20.short_description: PCA-weighted composite of access to care, segregation, and incarceration.
social_impact_profile_geo20.provenance: Reproduced from VDH HOI methodology using independently computed sub-indicators from Census ACS, LODES, EPA EJScreen, USDA FARA, CMS PUF, Vera Institute, and other sources. Each indicator is min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, then combined via VDH's PCA weight matrix to produce profile scores.
social_impact_profile_geo20.statement: The social impact profile score in {features.name} is {value}.

built_environment_profile_geo20.long_description: PCA-derived profile score measuring the natural, built, and social environment of a community. Computed from four min-max normalized indicators: environmental hazard index (inverted), population churning rate (inverted), population density, and walkability index. Weights are from the VDH HOI PCA weight matrix. Higher values indicate a more favorable built environment.
built_environment_profile_geo20.short_description: PCA-weighted composite of environmental hazard, population churning, density, and walkability.
built_environment_profile_geo20.provenance: Reproduced from VDH HOI methodology using independently computed sub-indicators from Census ACS, LODES, EPA EJScreen, USDA FARA, CMS PUF, Vera Institute, and other sources. Each indicator is min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, then combined via VDH's PCA weight matrix to produce profile scores.
built_environment_profile_geo20.statement: The built environment profile score in {features.name} is {value}.

health_opportunity_index_geo20.long_description: The Health Opportunity Index (HOI) is a composite index measuring social determinants of health at the census tract level. It combines 14 indicators into 4 profiles (Economic Opportunity, Consumer Opportunity, Social Impact, Built Environment) via PCA, then aggregates profiles into a single index using VDH's weight matrix. Higher values indicate greater health opportunity. Quintile labels (Very Low to Very High Opportunity) are assigned per year based on the distribution of tract-level scores.
health_opportunity_index_geo20.short_description: Composite index of social determinants of health from 14 indicators across 4 profiles.
health_opportunity_index_geo20.provenance: Reproduced from VDH HOI methodology using independently computed sub-indicators from Census ACS, LODES, EPA EJScreen, USDA FARA, CMS PUF, Vera Institute, and other sources. The 14 indicators are min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, multiplied by VDH's 14x4 PCA weight matrix to produce 4 profile scores, then combined with composite weights to produce the final HOI score. Methodology documented in docs/composite_indices_methodology.md.
health_opportunity_index_geo20.statement: The Health Opportunity Index in {features.name} is {value}.

marketplace_slcsp.long_description: The second-lowest-cost Silver plan (SLCSP) premium is the ACA marketplace benchmark that determines how much financial help a person receives when purchasing health insurance. Counties with higher SLCSP premiums have more expensive insurance markets, which can affect both the affordability of coverage and the out-of-pocket costs for residents who do not qualify for subsidies. Premiums are computed for a standardized 30-year-old non-tobacco-using individual to enable consistent geographic comparison.
marketplace_slcsp.provenance: Computed from CMS Public Use Files (PUFs) for ACA marketplace plans. Plan-level premium data from the Individual Market Medical PUF is filtered to Silver-tier plans and matched to counties via CMS Service Area PUFs. For each county and plan year, the second-lowest premium among available Silver plans is identified. Both Federally-Facilitated Marketplace (FFM) and State-Based Exchange (SBE) states are included. Premiums are for a 30-year-old non-tobacco-using individual using the base rate from the plan PUF.
marketplace_slcsp.short_description: Monthly premium for the ACA marketplace benchmark Silver plan (SLCSP) for a 30-year-old.

sources.location: Individual Market Medical PUF and Service Area PUF
sources.name: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS)

marketplace_slcsp.statement: The second-lowest-cost Silver plan monthly premium in {features.name} is ${value}.

marketplace_lcbp.long_description: The lowest-cost Bronze plan (LCBP) premium represents the cheapest ACA marketplace option with minimal monthly cost but higher out-of-pocket expenses when care is used. Counties where even the cheapest Bronze plan is expensive indicate insurance markets with limited competition or high underlying medical costs. This measure complements the Silver benchmark (SLCSP) by showing the floor of available coverage costs.
marketplace_lcbp.provenance: Computed from CMS Public Use Files (PUFs) for ACA marketplace plans. Plan-level premium data from the Individual Market Medical PUF is filtered to Bronze-tier plans and matched to counties via CMS Service Area PUFs. For each county and plan year, the lowest premium among available Bronze plans is identified. Both FFM and SBE states are included. Premiums are for a 30-year-old non-tobacco-using individual.
marketplace_lcbp.short_description: Monthly premium for the cheapest Bronze plan on the ACA marketplace for a 30-year-old.

sources.location: Individual Market Medical PUF and Service Area PUF
sources.name: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS)

marketplace_lcbp.statement: The lowest-cost Bronze plan monthly premium in {features.name} is ${value}.

perc_freq_mental_distress.long_description: Estimated percentage of adults reporting 14 or more days of poor mental health in the past 30 days. High prevalence of frequent mental distress reflects unmet behavioral health needs and is associated with reduced work productivity, strained social relationships, and increased demand for crisis services. Persistent mental distress at the neighborhood level often tracks with poverty, housing instability, and limited access to mental health providers.
perc_freq_mental_distress.short_description: Modeled prevalence of adults with 14+ poor mental health days per month, from BRFSS small-area estimation.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

perc_freq_mental_distress.provenance: Tract-level estimates from CDC PLACES (Population Level Analysis and Community Estimates), which applies multilevel regression with poststratification (MRP) to Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) survey responses. Each model year pools two years of BRFSS data with Census tract demographics to generate small-area predictions. Values are crude prevalence (%). Confidence intervals converted from 95% CI (PLACES default) to approximate 90% MOE using the ratio 1.645/1.96.
perc_freq_mental_distress.statement: {value}% of adults in {features.name} report frequent mental distress.

perc_freq_physical_distress.long_description: Estimated percentage of adults reporting 14 or more days of poor physical health in the past 30 days. Frequent physical distress captures chronic pain, disability, and illness burden at the community level. Neighborhoods with high rates often face compounding challenges including limited healthcare access, environmental hazards, and higher proportions of residents in physically demanding occupations.
perc_freq_physical_distress.short_description: Modeled prevalence of adults with 14+ poor physical health days per month, from BRFSS small-area estimation.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

Confidence intervals converted from 95% CI to approximate 90% MOE.
perc_freq_physical_distress.statement: {value}% of adults in {features.name} report frequent physical distress.

adequateplus_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother received more than the recommended number of prenatal visits, beginning in the first four months of pregnancy (110% or more of expected visits). High adequate-plus rates may reflect intensive monitoring of high-risk pregnancies or provider practice patterns. The Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) Index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index, evaluates prenatal care by combining when care began with the number of visits relative to ACOG-recommended schedules.
adequateplus_pc.long_name: Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization: Adequate Plus
adequateplus_pc.short_description: Proportion of births with prenatal care exceeding 110% of recommended visits, starting by the 4th month.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

adequateplus_pc.provenance: Prenatal care adequacy is measured using the Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index (Kotelchuck, M., 1994). Birth records are queried from CDC WONDER Natality Expanded (D149) by county, month, and number of prenatal visits. The index classifies each birth into four categories based on the month prenatal care began and the ratio of observed to expected visits (expected visits defined by ACOG guidelines). This pipeline assumes 39-week gestational age when computing expected visits, as WONDER does not report gestational age in cross-tabulations.
adequateplus_pc.statement: {value}% of births in {features.name} had adequate-plus prenatal care.

adequate_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother received adequate prenatal care — starting in the first four months and completing 80-109% of recommended visits. This is the target level of prenatal care utilization. Counties with high adequate rates have maternal health systems that successfully engage pregnant women early and maintain recommended visit schedules through delivery. The Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) Index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index, evaluates prenatal care by combining when care began with the number of visits relative to ACOG-recommended schedules.

inadequate_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother received inadequate prenatal care — either starting after the fourth month of pregnancy, receiving fewer than 50% of recommended visits, or having no prenatal care at all. High inadequate rates signal systemic barriers to maternal health services such as provider shortages, lack of insurance, or geographic isolation. Inadequate prenatal care is associated with higher rates of preterm birth, low birth weight, and maternal complications. vi_overall.long_description: The Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) is a composite measure that ranks census tracts on their relative vulnerability to public health emergencies and natural disasters. It integrates 16 social factors from U.S. Census data across four themes: socioeconomic status, household characteristics, racial and ethnic minority status, and housing type and transportation. Communities with high SVI scores face greater challenges in preparing for, responding to, and recovering from hazardous events. The index helps public health officials and emergency planners direct resources to populations most likely to need support during crises.
vi_overall.short_description: The Social Vulnerability Index comprised of four themes.

sources.name: American Community Survey / CDC ATSDR SVI Methodology

vi_overall.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates using the CDC/ATSDR SVI 2022 methodology. Sixteen social factors from ACS tables (B/S/DP) are converted to percentages, percentile-ranked within the region (VA or NCR), summed by theme, and re-ranked. Uses B-table variables (B03002, B09001, B11012, B18101) for cross-year stability instead of DP05/DP02 which renumber across ACS vintages. Validated against CDC SVI 2020 at r>0.998 for all themes.
vi_overall.statement: The overall Social Vulnerability Index for {feature.name} is {value}.

vi_socioeconomic.long_description: The socioeconomic status theme of the Social Vulnerability Index captures community-level economic disadvantage using Census indicators such as poverty rate, unemployment, per capita income, and educational attainment. Tracts with high socioeconomic vulnerability face compounding disadvantages that limit residents ability to prepare for and recover from emergencies. Economic hardship restricts access to transportation, healthcare, and safe housing during disaster events. This theme is one of four components that contribute to the overall SVI score.
vi_socioeconomic.short_description: Socioeconomic status theme for calculating Social Vulnerability Index.

sources.name: American Community Survey / CDC ATSDR SVI Methodology

vi_socioeconomic.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates using the CDC/ATSDR SVI 2022 methodology. Sixteen social factors from ACS tables (B/S/DP) are converted to percentages, percentile-ranked within the region (VA or NCR), summed by theme, and re-ranked. Uses B-table variables (B03002, B09001, B11012, B18101) for cross-year stability instead of DP05/DP02 which renumber across ACS vintages. Validated against CDC SVI 2020 at r>0.998 for all themes.

vi_household.long_description: The household characteristics theme of the Social Vulnerability Index measures demographic factors that increase vulnerability, including the proportion of residents aged 65 and older, aged 17 and under, with disabilities, and single-parent households. These populations often require additional assistance during evacuations and have specialized medical or caregiving needs that complicate disaster response. High scores on this theme indicate communities where a larger share of residents may be unable to shelter in place or evacuate independently. This theme is one of four components that contribute to the overall SVI score.
vi_household.short_description: Housing characteristics index theme for calculating Social Vulnerability Index.

sources.name: American Community Survey / CDC ATSDR SVI Methodology

vi_household.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates using the CDC/ATSDR SVI 2022 methodology. Sixteen social factors from ACS tables (B/S/DP) are converted to percentages, percentile-ranked within the region (VA or NCR), summed by theme, and re-ranked. Uses B-table variables (B03002, B09001, B11012, B18101) for cross-year stability instead of DP05/DP02 which renumber across ACS vintages. Validated against CDC SVI 2020 at r>0.998 for all themes.
vi_household.statement: The Housing characteristics index for {feature.name} is {value}.

vi_race_ethnicity.long_description: The racial and ethnic minority status theme of the Social Vulnerability Index captures the proportion of residents who identify as racial or ethnic minorities and those with limited English proficiency. Historical patterns of residential segregation and disinvestment mean that minority communities often face disproportionate exposure to environmental hazards and reduced access to emergency resources. Language barriers can further impede receipt of evacuation warnings and access to recovery assistance. This theme is one of four components that contribute to the overall SVI score.

vi_housing_transportation.long_description: The housing type and transportation theme of the Social Vulnerability Index measures structural vulnerability through indicators such as multi-unit housing, mobile homes, crowding, lack of vehicle access, and group quarters populations. Residents in multi-unit or mobile housing face heightened risks during natural disasters, while those without vehicles may be unable to evacuate. Crowded living conditions can accelerate disease transmission during public health emergencies. This theme is one of four components that contribute to the overall SVI score.

avg_monthly_rate.category: Behavioral Health, Substance Use Disorder and Recovery
avg_monthly_rate.long_description: Average monthly emergency department visits for drug overdose per 100,000 residents. This measure captures the burden of acute drug overdose events on local emergency medical systems and serves as a near-real-time indicator of substance use crises in a community. Tracking ED visit rates enables public health officials to identify emerging overdose hotspots, allocate naloxone distribution resources, and evaluate the impact of harm reduction interventions. Elevated rates may reflect increased opioid availability, changes in illicit drug supply composition, or gaps in substance use treatment access.
avg_monthly_rate.long_name: Monthly average emergency department visits for drug overdose rate per 100,000 residents
avg_monthly_rate.short_description: Average monthly rate of emergency department visits for drug overdose per 100,000 residents.
avg_monthly_rate.short_name: Monthly average ED visits for drug overdose

avg_monthly_rate.provenance: Emergency department visit rates for drug overdose from Virginia Department of Health surveillance data. Monthly counts aggregated to average monthly rate per 100,000 population by health district.

prevent_hosp_rate.long_description: Rate of hospital stays for ambulatory-care sensitive conditions per 100,000 Medicare enrollees. Ambulatory-care sensitive conditions are diagnoses that are usually treatable in outpatient settings, such as diabetes, asthma, and hypertension. High rates of preventable hospitalizations indicate inadequate access to primary care or gaps in chronic disease management, placing unnecessary burden on the hospital system.
prevent_hosp_rate.long_name: Preventable hospital stays per 100,000 Medicare enrollees
prevent_hosp_rate.short_description: Rate of preventable hospital stays for ambulatory-care sensitive conditions per 100,000 Medicare enrollees.

affordability_index_geo20.short_description: Proportion of income spent on combined housing and transportation costs for a Regional Typical Household.
affordability_index_geo20.long_description: The Housing + Transportation (H+T) Affordability Index measures the true cost of housing by combining housing and transportation costs as a percentage of household income. A neighborhood is considered affordable when H+T costs consume no more than 45% of household income. This measure uses the Regional Typical Household variant, which reflects a household earning the regional median income. Reproduced independently using CNT's published regression coefficients (Tables 3-6) applied to ACS demographic data, LEHD employment data, and GTFS transit schedule data at block group resolution.
affordability_index_geo20.statement: In {features.name}, combined housing and transportation costs consume {value}% of a typical household's income.
affordability_index_geo20.provenance: Independently reproduced using the methodology published by the Center for Neighborhood Technology (CNT). Regression coefficients from CNT Tables 3-6 are applied to 17 independent variables computed from American Community Survey data, LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics, TIGER geographic data, and GTFS transit feeds. Block group estimates are aggregated to tract and county via unweighted means.

monthly_rent_0br.long_description: Fair Market Rent (FMR) represents the 40th percentile of gross rents for typical, non-substandard rental units in a market area. Studio FMRs set the baseline for housing assistance programs and reflect the minimum cost of independent housing. Areas where even studio rents are high relative to incomes face acute affordability challenges for single-person households and individuals transitioning out of homelessness.
monthly_rent_0br.provenance: Fair Market Rents estimated by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) for metropolitan areas and nonmetropolitan counties, FY2019–FY2026. County-level values are population-weighted averages of ZIP-code-level Small Area Fair Market Rents (SAFMRs) using ZCTA-county population intersection weights from the 2010 Census. Tract-level values are population-weighted averages of ZIP-level SAFMRs using the 2021 Q4 HUD USPS ZIP-to-tract crosswalk, with fallback to county-level FMR where ZIP data is unavailable.
monthly_rent_0br.short_description: Fair Market Rent for a studio (0-bedroom) dwelling.

incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.long_description: The incarceration rate estimates how many people per 100,000 residents are held in jail, allocated to the census tracts where they lived before incarceration. Communities with high rates experience disproportionate removal of residents into the criminal justice system, which disrupts families, reduces household income, and weakens social networks. Only jail population is captured; state prison data is not available at the county level for Virginia.
incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.short_description: Jail incarceration rate per 100,000 residents, allocated to tracts by pre-incarceration residence.
incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.provenance: Tract-level incarceration counts from Prison Policy Initiative (PPI) 2020, which estimates pre-incarceration residence using Bureau of Justice Statistics survey data applied to 2020 Census group quarters. County-level annual jail population from Vera Institute (mean of quarterly observations, 2016-2023). PPI 2020 tract counts scaled by Vera year-over-year county jail population change relative to 2020 baseline. Rates computed as scaled count / PPI total population * 100,000. Virginia uses regional jails shared across counties; Vera attributes populations back to individual counties. 129 of 133 VA counties have Vera data; remaining 4 use unscaled PPI 2020 values for all years. PPI data uses 2020 census tracts natively; standardize_all appends geo suffixes.
incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.statement: The incarceration rate per 100,000 in {features.name} is {value}.

commute_time_geo20.long_description: Self-reported mean travel time to work for workers aged 16 and older who did not work from home. Commute time is a key indicator of transportation infrastructure adequacy and residential-employment spatial mismatch. Long commutes reduce time available for family, civic engagement, and physical activity, and are associated with higher rates of obesity, stress, and cardiovascular disease. Trends in commute time also reflect shifts in remote work adoption and regional housing affordability pressures.
commute_time_geo20.short_description: Average one-way travel time to work in minutes for workers aged 16 and older who did not work from home.

perc_carpool_geo20.long_description: Percentage of workers aged 16 and older who commute to work by carpooling. Carpooling rates reflect the availability and attractiveness of shared transportation options in a community. Higher carpool rates can reduce traffic congestion, lower per-capita vehicle emissions, and decrease household transportation costs. Low carpool rates may indicate dispersed employment centers, limited ride-matching infrastructure, or work schedules that are difficult to coordinate.

perc_no_vehicle_geo20.long_description: Percentage of occupied housing units with no vehicle available. Vehicle access is a critical determinant of mobility, employment opportunity, and access to healthcare and essential services in areas with limited public transit. Communities with high rates of zero-vehicle households face disproportionate transportation barriers that can reinforce economic isolation. This measure helps identify areas where investments in transit, bike infrastructure, or ride-sharing programs are most needed.

walkability_index_geo20.long_description: The Walkability Index is a composite measure that ranks block groups according to their relative walkability, computed annually for 2017-2023. It combines four components: (1) D2A_Ranked — employment and household land use entropy from LEHD LODES WAC job counts by NAICS sector and ACS B11001 household counts, measuring the diversity of nearby land uses; (2) D2B_Ranked — employment-only entropy across five tiers (retail, office, industrial, service, entertainment); (3) D3B_Ranked — street intersection density from the EPA Smart Location Database V3, measuring pedestrian-friendly street connectivity; and (4) D4C_Ranked — distance to the nearest transit stop computed from national GTFS feeds via the Mobility Database, measuring transit accessibility. Each component is ranked into 20 quantile bins across all block groups in the coverage area. The final index is calculated as NatWalkInd = D2A_Ranked/6 + D2B_Ranked/6 + D3B_Ranked/3 + D4C_Ranked/3, producing scores from 1 (least walkable) to 20 (most walkable). Block group scores are aggregated to Census tracts and counties using population-weighted means.
walkability_index_geo20.short_description: Composite walkability score (1-20) combining employment entropy, street connectivity, and transit proximity, updated annually from LODES, EPA SLD, and GTFS data.

walkability_index_geo20.provenance: Derived from the EPA National Walkability Index methodology. Employment entropy (D2A/D2B) computed annually from LEHD LODES + ACS data. Street connectivity (D3B) from EPA SLD V3. Transit proximity (D4C) computed annually from national GTFS feeds. Formula: NatWalkInd = D2A_Ranked/6 + D2B_Ranked/6 + D3B_Ranked/3 + D4C_Ranked/3.
